_note: Starter AP Chemistry FRQs with correct worked physics/chem. courseId must match a course id in registry.json. difficulty is 1 (easy) – 3 (hard); points are per part. Expand freely.

questions.title: Limiting reactant in ammonia synthesis
questions.scenario: For N₂(g) + 3 H₂(g) → 2 NH₃(g), a reactor is charged with 28 g of N₂ and 10 g of H₂. Use molar masses N₂ = 28 g/mol, H₂ = 2.0 g/mol, NH₃ = 17 g/mol.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.modelAnswer: n(N₂) = 28/28 = 1.0 mol; n(H₂) = 10/2.0 = 5.0 mol. 1.0 mol N₂ needs 3.0 mol H₂, and 5.0 mol H₂ is available, so H₂ is in excess and N₂ is limiting.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Calculate the mass of NH₃ produced.
parts.modelAnswer: From 1.0 mol N₂ → 2.0 mol NH₃. Mass = 2.0 × 17 = 34 g NH₃.

parts.label: Part (c)
parts.text: How many moles of the excess reactant remain?
parts.modelAnswer: H₂ used = 3 × 1.0 = 3.0 mol; remaining = 5.0 − 3.0 = 2.0 mol H₂.

questions.title: Partial pressures in a gas mixture
questions.scenario: A rigid 2.0 L vessel at 300 K holds 0.50 mol O₂ and 0.50 mol N₂. Use R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K).

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the total pressure in the vessel.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Calculate the partial pressure of O₂.

questions.title: Calorimetry of a dissolving salt
questions.scenario: Dissolving 0.010 mol of a salt in 50.0 g of water raises the temperature from 25.0 °C to 45.0 °C. Assume the solution has the specific heat of water, c = 4.18 J/(g·°C).

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the heat absorbed by the water.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Determine the molar enthalpy of solution and state whether it is exo- or endothermic.
parts.modelAnswer: Heat came from the dissolving process, so it is exothermic. ΔH = −4.18 kJ / 0.010 mol = −418 kJ/mol.

questions.title: Energy of an emitted photon
questions.scenario: A hydrogen atom emits light of wavelength λ = 486 nm. Use h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s and c = 3.00×10⁸ m/s.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the frequency of the light.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Calculate the energy of one photon.

questions.title: First-order decomposition
questions.scenario: A reactant decomposes by first-order kinetics with rate constant k = 0.0231 s⁻¹.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the half-life of the reaction.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: How long until only 25% of the reactant remains?

questions.title: ICE table for HI formation
questions.scenario: For H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2 HI(g), Kc = 50.0 at the reaction temperature. A flask initially contains [H₂] = [I₂] = 1.00 M and [HI] = 0.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Set up an ICE table in terms of the change x.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Solve for the equilibrium concentration of HI.

questions.title: pH of a weak acid
questions.scenario: A 0.10 M solution of a weak monoprotic acid HA has Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵.

questions.title: Standard cell potential and free energy
questions.scenario: A galvanic cell is built from Zn²⁺/Zn (E° = −0.76 V) and Cu²⁺/Cu (E° = +0.34 V). Use F = 96485 C/mol.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the standard cell potential and identify the cathode.
parts.modelAnswer: Cu²⁺/Cu is the cathode (higher reduction potential). E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = 0.34 − (−0.76) = +1.10 V.

questions.title: Acid–base titration
questions.scenario: A 25.0 mL sample of HCl is exactly neutralized by 32.0 mL of 0.100 M NaOH.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the moles of NaOH used.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Determine the molarity of the HCl.
parts.modelAnswer: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O is 1:1, so n(HCl) = 3.20×10⁻³ mol. [HCl] = 3.20×10⁻³ / 0.0250 = 0.128 M.
